questions.q: ${ }_{92}^{238} \mathrm{U}$ emits $8 \alpha$-particles and $6 \beta$-particles. The neutron/proton ratio in the product nucleus is
questions.solution:
${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238} \stackrel{-8 \alpha}{-6 \beta}{ }_{82} X^{206}$
Number of protons $=82$
Number of neutrons $=124$
Neutron/proton ratio in the product nucleus $=\frac{124}{82}=\frac{62}{41}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.q:
Spin only magnetic moment 2.84 BM is given by:

(Atomic Number of Ni=28,Ti=22,Cr=24,Co=27 )
questions.solution:
μ= n( n+2 ) =2.84⇒n=2

electronic configuration of Ni +2 =1 s 2 2 s 2 2 p 6 3 s 2 3 p 6 3 d 8   e − having two unpaired electrons

electron configuration of T i 3+ =1 s 2 2 s 2 2 p 6 3 s 2 3 p 6 3 d 1 only one unpaired electron

electron configuration of C r 2+ =1 s 2 2 s 2 2 p 6 3 s 2 3 p 6 3 d 4 having four unpaired electrons

electron configuration of C o +2 =1 s 2 2 s 2 2 p 6 3 s 2 3 p 6 3 d 7 having three unpaired electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The energy required to break one mole of hydrogen-hydrogen bonds in $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is $436 \mathrm{kJ}$. What is the longest wavelength of light required to break a single hydrogenhydrogen bond?
questions.solution:
Given, energy of one mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ bonds $=436 \mathrm{k} \mathrm{J}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\because E=\frac{n \cdot h c}{\lambda}\\
\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{n.h c}{E} \\
\lambda=\frac{6.634 \times 10^{-} 34 \mathrm{s} \times 3 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{ms}^{-1} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{436 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{J}} \mathrm{m} \\
=0.2736 \times 10^{-34+8+23-3} \\
=0.2736 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m} \\
=273.6 \mathrm{nm}=274 \mathrm{nm}
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following statement is incorrect?
options: Isobars possess the same chemical properties.
Isotopes occupy the same position in the periodic table.
Isotopes possess the same atomic number.
In isobars, the total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus is same.
questions.solution: Isobars have the same mass number with the same physical properties and differ in the chemical properties.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of these species will have non-zero magnetic moment?
questions.solution:
Key point The species which has one or more unpaired electrons have non-zero magnetic moment.
Electronic configuration of
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Na}^{+}(Z=11)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 p^{6} \\
\mathrm{Mg}(Z=12)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 p^{6} 3 \mathrm{s}^{2} \\
\mathrm{F}^{-}(Z=9)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 s^{2} 2 p^{6} \\
\quad \mathrm{Ar}^{+}(Z=18)=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 p^{6} 3 \mathrm{s}^{2} 3 p^{5}
\end{array}
$$
As, Ar $^{+}$ has an unpaired electron, it has non-zero magnetic moment. Hence, (d) is the correct option.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: According to the Bohr theory, which of the following transitions in the hydrogen atom will give rise to the least energetic photon?
questions.solution: $\because \Delta E \propto\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]$, where $n_2 > n_1$ Energy of photon obtained from the transition $n=6$ to $n=5$ will have least energy.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following about an electron occupying the 1s orbital in a hydrogen atom is incorrect? (The Bohr radius is represented by <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>).
options: The total energy of the electron is maximum when it is at a distance <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> from the nucleus.
The electron can be found at a distance <math><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> from the nucleus.
The probability density of finding the electron is maximum at the nucleus.
The magnitude of the potential energy is double that of its kinetic energy on an average.
questions.solution: From the graph it is clear that probability of finding electron is maximum at the nucleus
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

b. Cr3+ ii. 35 B.M.
c. Fe3+ iii. 3 B.M.

  v. 15B.M
options: <math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>
questions.solution:
Magnetic moment μ=nn+2 B.M.
(i) Co3+→1s22s22p63s23p64s03d6

n=4
μ= 44+2=24 B.M.
(ii) Cr+3→1s22s22p63s23p64s03d3

n=3
μ= 33+2= 15 B.M.
(iii) Fe3+→1s22s22p63s23p64s03d5

n=5
μ= 55+2= 35 B.M.
(iv) Ni+2→1s22s22p63s23p64s03d8

n=2
μ= 22+2= 8 B.M.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Impossible orbital among the following is
questions.solution:
According to Bohr - Burry's scheme, Maximum number of $\mathrm{e}^{-}$in 3 rd orbital $=3 \mathrm{n}^{2}$
$$
=3(3)^{2}=18
$$
and maximum number of $\mathrm{e}^{-}$in
s-subshell $=2$
p-subshell $=6$
d-subshell $=10$
f-subshell $=14$
Therefore, $18 \mathrm{e}^{-}$present in 3rd orbital enter in s, $\mathrm{p}$ and $\mathrm{d}$ subshell. Thereafter, there is no $\mathrm{e}^{-}$ available for f-subshell. That's why $3 \mathrm{f}$ subshell is not possible.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following corresponds to a photon of highest energy?
questions.solution:
$\because E=h v=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=h c \cdot \bar{v} \quad\left(\because \frac{1}{\lambda}=\bar{v}\right)$
where $E=$ energy of photon 
$c=$ velocity of photon (-light) 
$\lambda=$ wavelength of photon 
$h=$ plank's constant.
$\therefore$ For $(\mathrm{a})$
$E=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{4} / 300 \times 10^{-9}$
$E=1.98 \times 10^{-25} \cdot \mathrm{J} / 300 \times 10^{-9}$
$\therefore \quad E=\frac{1.98 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{J}}{300 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}$
$(a) \rightarrow E=6.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}$
For (b) $E=h v=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{4}$
$(b) \rightarrow E=1.98 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{J}$
For (c) $E=h c \times v$
$\because \frac{1}{3}$
$$
E=6.63 \times 10^{-4} \times 3 \times 10^{*} \times 30 \times 10^{2}
$$
(c) $\Rightarrow E=5.96 \times 10^{\circ}$
For $(d) d \rightarrow E=6.62 \times 10^{-27} J$
Hence, highest energy for photon is in (a).
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: In a sample of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>-</mo></math>atom, electrons make transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>5</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> excited state to ground state, producing all possible types of photons, then number of lines in infrared region are:
questions.solution:
Infrared lines = Total lines - visible lines -UV lines =6(6-1)2-4-5  =15-9=6

(visible lines =4  6→2,5→2,4→2,3→2)

(UV lines=5  6→1,5→1,4→1,3→1,2→1)
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The orbital angular momentum of an electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> orbital is
questions.solution:
For s-subshell l=0

Orbital angular momentum (mvr)=h2πl(l+1)

For 2s orbital, l (azimuthal quantum number) =0
∴ orbital angular momentum =h2π0(0+1)

=h2π0=0

Thus, the orbital angular momentum of an electron in 2s -orbital is Zero.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If the radius of $\mathrm{H}$ is $0.53 Ã…$, then what will be the radius of ${ }_{3} \mathrm{Li}^{2+}$?
questions.solution:
Radius of orbit $=\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{z}}\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{o}}=0.529 \mathrm{~A}\right)$
Radius of $\mathrm{H}=\frac{(1)^{2} \times 0.529 \mathrm{~A}}{1}=0.53 \mathrm{~A}$
Thus, the radius of ${ }_{3} \mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ will be $=\frac{(1)^{2} \times 0.529}{3}=0.17 Ã…$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Paragraph: The hydrogen-like species $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ is in a spherically symmetric state $S_1$ with one radial node. Upon absorbing light the ion undergoes transition to a state $S_2$. The state $S_2$ has one radial node and its energy is equal to the ground state energy of the hydrogen atom.Question: The state $S_1$ is
questions.solution:
Given : Hydrogen like species, $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$
Spherically symmetric state, $S_1 \Rightarrow s$-orbital
$S_1$ has radial node $1 \Rightarrow 2 s \quad$ (No. of radial node $=1=n-l-1$, so $n=2$ ) Species undergoes state $S_2$
$S_2$ has energy equal to the ground state energy of $\mathrm{H}$-atom $=-13.6 \mathrm{eV}$
So, $\quad-13.6=-13.6\left(\frac{3^2}{n^2}\right)$
$n=3$
Again radial node $=1=n-1-1$
or $1=3-l-1 \quad$ so $\quad l=1$
It suggests $S_2$ is $3 p$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: According to Bohr's theory $E_n=$ Total energy, $K_n=$ Kinetic energy, $V_n=$ Potential energy, $r_n=$ Radius of $n$th orbit
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's theory,
Total energy is $E_n=K_n+V_n$
Kinetic energy $=K_n=\frac{1}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r}$
Potential energy $=V_n=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r}$
$\therefore \quad E_n=\frac{1}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r}$
Radius of $n$th orbit $\left(r_n\right)=\frac{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m Z e^2}$ $$ E_n=-\frac{m e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \times \frac{1}{n^2} $$ (A) $\frac{V_n}{K_n}=\frac{-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r}}{\frac{1}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r}}=-2$
Hence, $(A)$ match with $(R)$.
(B) $E_n \propto \frac{1}{n^2}$ or $E_n \propto \frac{1}{r_n}$
Radius of $n$th orbit $r_n \propto E_n^x$ $\therefore \quad x=-1$
Hence, (B) match with (Q).
(C) Angular momentum $=\frac{h}{2 \pi} \sqrt{l(l+1) l}=0,1,2, \ldots$
For the lower orbit $n=1$
$\therefore \quad l=0$ and $m=0$
Hence, angular momentum of lowest orbit $=\frac{h}{2 \pi} \sqrt{0(0 H)}=0$
(C) match with (P)
(D) $\frac{1}{r^n} \propto Z^y$ as $r_n \propto \frac{1}{Z} \therefore y=1$
Hence, (D) match with (S).
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: 1 mole of photon, each of frequency $2500 \mathrm{~S}^{-1}$, would have approximately a total energy of:
questions.solution: Total Energy $=\mathrm{Nhv}=6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} . \mathrm{S} . \times 2500 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}=9.9 \mathrm{erg} \approx 10 \mathrm{erg}$ In (A) option, it should be 10 erg instead of $1 \mathrm{erg}$.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
The quantum number of four electrons are given below:
I.  n=4, l=2, ml=–2, ms=–1/2
II.  n=3, l=2, ml=1, ms=+1/2
III. n=4, l=1, ml=0, ms=+1/2
IV. n=3, l=1, ml=1, ms=–1/2
The correct order of their increasing energies will be:
questions.solution:
Higher the value of n+l higher will be energy.
Hence, 3p4<3d5<4p5<4d7
If n+l same, then higher the value of, higher will be energy.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
